model_narrative: This is a permutation flow-shop scheduling problem. A set of jobs must each pass through every machine in one fixed machine order, and all machines process the jobs in the same single sequence. We decide the order in which the jobs are processed and the resulting start and finish times on each machine. The objective is to make the makespan as small as possible, meaning the time at which the last job clears the last machine.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the jobs to be processed

sets.name: m
sets.doc: the machines, listed in the fixed processing order; every job visits the machines in this same order, starting at the first machine listed and finishing at the last

sets.name: k
sets.doc: the slots in the processing sequence, listed from first to last; the job placed in an earlier slot is processed before the job in a later slot, and all machines use this one common job ordering

params.name: proctime
params.doc: the time a machine needs to process a job, in time units, given for each machine and job pair

vars.name: rank
vars.doc: 1 if the job is placed in the given sequence slot, 0 otherwise

vars.name: start
vars.doc: the time the machine begins working on whichever job occupies the given sequence slot

vars.name: comp
vars.doc: the time the machine finishes the job that occupies the given sequence slot

vars.name: totwait
vars.doc: the makespan, meaning the moment the very last job leaves the very last machine

constraints.name: rank[i,
constraints.intent: Each slot in the processing sequence must hold exactly one job. For every slot, exactly one job is assigned to occupy it.

constraints.name: rank[i,
constraints.intent: Each job must take exactly one place in the processing sequence. For every job, it is assigned to exactly one slot.

constraints.name:
k)
def
constraints.intent: On any one machine, work on consecutive slots cannot overlap. For each machine and each slot that has a following slot, the machine may not begin the job in the next slot until it has finished the job in the current slot.

constraints.name:
m)
def
constraints.intent: A job must clear one machine before the next machine in the processing order can begin working on it. For each slot and each machine that has a following machine, the start of that slot's job on the next machine cannot come before its finish on the current machine.

constraints.name: comp[m,
constraints.intent: On each machine, the finish time of the job in a slot equals its start time on that machine plus the time that machine needs to process whichever job occupies that slot.

constraints.name:
m)[-1]
lastrank
constraints.intent: The makespan is no earlier than the finish time of the job in the last slot on the last machine in the processing order.

query: There's a proposal on the table to lead the run with our two heaviest pieces — parts i4 and i6 — by having both of them land somewhere in the first two positions of the sequence, so the long jobs go through the line up front. What does the optimizer return for the overall finish time?

query: We're looking at a front-loading idea: get our three lightest parts — i1, i2 and i3 — all done inside the first three positions of the sequence so the floor clears the quick ones up front. Plug that in and walk me through the resulting makespan.
expected_pyomo: model.light_front = Constraint(expr=sum(model.rank['i1', k] + model.rank['i2', k] + model.rank['i3', k] for k in ['i1', 'i2', 'i3']) >= 3)

query: Engineering is pitching a paced release at the bending machine — each successive job in the sequence shouldn't start there until at least six minutes after the one before it, to give the operator breathing room. What's the resulting total run time once that pacing is in?

query: Part i6 is heavy and it keeps landing ahead of the quick part i2 in the schedule, which feels backwards on the floor. Shouldn't i6 always come later in the sequence than i2 so the light one clears first? What's keeping the model from ordering them that way?
expected_pyomo: model.i6_after_i2 = Constraint(expr=sum((p + 1) * model.rank['i6', ['i1', 'i2', 'i3', 'i4', 'i5', 'i6'][p]] for p in range(6)) >= sum((p + 1) * model.rank['i2', ['i1', 'i2', 'i3', 'i4', 'i5', 'i6'][p]] for p in range(6)) + 1)

query: Our two heaviest pieces, i4 and i6, are scattered through the middle of the run where they hold everything up. Wouldn't it be cleaner to push both of them into the last two positions so the line streams the light work first? What's the trade-off pulling them back toward the middle?
expected_pyomo: model.heavy_back = Constraint(expr=sum(model.rank['i4', k] + model.rank['i6', k] for k in ['i5', 'i6']) >= 2)

query: Assembly seems to sit idle early in the run, then everything piles up at the back end. Shouldn't each job's assembly completion build up steadily — say no earlier than seven minutes times its position in the sequence — so the load spreads out instead of bunching? What's the model seeing that pulls it away from a paced finish like that?

query: On the floor we'd naturally chain our heavy parts so i6 leads i4 and i4 in turn leads i1 — work the big jobs through in that order rather than letting them float. The plan ignores that chain entirely. Couldn't we run them in that sequence? Walk me through what's pulling it the other way.
expected_pyomo:
model.chain_i6_i4 = Constraint(expr=sum((p + 1) * model.rank['i6', ['i1', 'i2', 'i3', 'i4', 'i5', 'i6'][p]] for p in range(6)) + 1 <= sum((p + 1) * model.rank['i4', ['i1', 'i2', 'i3', 'i4', 'i5', 'i6'][p]] for p in range(6)))
model.chain_i4_i1 = Constraint(expr=sum((p + 1) * model.rank['i4', ['i1', 'i2', 'i3', 'i4', 'i5', 'i6'][p]] for p in range(6)) + 1 <= sum((p + 1) * model.rank['i1', ['i1', 'i2', 'i3', 'i4', 'i5', 'i6'][p]] for p in range(6)))
